name: table fork
shortDescription: A plain table fork with serviceable tines, made for meals respectable enough to avoid fingers but not grand enough to require silver. Inns, kitchens, and traveling households buy them in numbers because forks disappear faster than anyone admits.
flavorText: “The fork brought manners to supper and inventory loss to innkeepers.”

name: table knife
shortDescription: A small table knife meant for bread, cheese, fruit, and the civilized division of cooked meals. Its edge is useful rather than threatening, though tavern owners still count them carefully after guests argue over bills.
flavorText: “Polite steel is still steel enough to make a landlord nervous.”

name: table spoon
shortDescription: A sturdy table spoon for stews, porridges, sauces, and all the foods that refuse to behave on a fork. It is humble dining ware, but every cook knows a meal feels poorer when customers must chase soup with bread crusts.
flavorText: “A spoon is proof that broth deserves dignity.”

name: tea spoon
shortDescription: A small spoon for tea, sugar, medicines, spices, and careful measures that become arguments when guessed. Apothecaries and tea houses both prize it, though one uses it for comfort and the other for charges by the pinch.
flavorText: “Tiny spoons make tiny quantities look deliberate.”

name: chopsticks
shortDescription: A neat pair of chopsticks traded through ports, tea houses, and foreign quarters where skill at the table is part of the meal. They are light, cheap to ship, and surprisingly elegant in the hands of someone who knows what they are doing.
flavorText: “Two sticks can reveal a traveler faster than an accent.”

name: curtain
shortDescription: A hanging curtain used to soften rooms, hide storage, divide sleeping spaces, or keep the afternoon glare off goods that fade. It is cloth with social talent, turning one room into several and a shop window into a small performance.
flavorText: “A curtain is a wall that changes its mind.”

name: rough pillow
shortDescription: A rough pillow stuffed with plain fiber, straw, or whatever the maker trusted more than comfort. It suits barracks, cheap inns, wagons, and anyone too tired to bargain with their own neck after a long road.
flavorText: “Not soft, exactly, but softer than pride.”

name: nice pillow
shortDescription: A pleasant pillow with better stuffing and cleaner cloth, the sort of small luxury that makes a rented room feel almost kind. Innkeepers buy them to raise rates, and travelers buy them after waking with shoulders full of regret.
flavorText: “Comfort often begins as something small under the head.”

name: nice large pillow
shortDescription: A large comfortable pillow, generously stuffed and made for beds that promise real rest rather than temporary collapse. It is bulky to pack but easy to sell to households, guesthouses, and anyone who believes sleep should have weight and softness.
flavorText: “A large pillow turns surrender into a household decision.”

name: thin blanket
shortDescription: A thin blanket for mild nights, travel rolls, spare beds, and rooms where warmth is needed but coin is short. It folds small, dries quickly, and becomes more valuable the moment a guest realizes the alternative is none at all.
flavorText: “Thin warmth is still warmer than complaint.”

name: thick blanket
shortDescription: A heavy blanket woven for cold rooms, winter carts, guard posts, and anyone who has learned that fireplaces do not reach every corner. It is plain comfort with strong trade value wherever frost turns sleep into a negotiation.
flavorText: “The best blankets make weather feel less personal.”

name: fan
shortDescription: A folding fan for warm halls, crowded courts, kitchens, and discreet gestures made behind painted ribs. It cools the hand, hides a glance, and gives fashionable buyers something to move while pretending not to listen.
flavorText: “A fan stirs air, gossip, and suspicion with equal grace.”

name: fancy bowl
shortDescription: A decorative bowl with better glaze, shape, or ornament than common kitchen ware strictly needs. It can hold fruit, soup, sweets, offerings, or nothing at all, which is how wealthy houses prove they own enough bowls for display.
flavorText: “Fine bowls often serve the eye before the meal.”

name: fancy plate
shortDescription: A refined plate made for formal tables, rich servings, and owners who believe food tastes better when framed by good craft. It sells well in sets, but single pieces still tempt collectors trying to replace what guests broke quietly.
flavorText: “A fancy plate makes even crumbs feel accountable.”

name: fondue pot
shortDescription: A fondue pot for melted cheese, chocolate, sauces, or other shared comforts kept warm over a small flame. Taverns treat it as novelty, nobles treat it as amusement, and merchants treat it as several customers leaning over one profitable dish.
flavorText: “Communal food is just commerce with longer forks.”

name: tea cup
shortDescription: A small tea cup shaped for hot drinks, quiet rooms, delicate fingers, and conversations that last longer than the tea itself. Tea sellers know cups matter because aroma, color, and status all need somewhere graceful to sit.
flavorText: “A cup of tea is cheaper than peace and often confused for it.”

name: wooden cup
shortDescription: A simple wooden cup carved for camps, kitchens, road inns, and workers who prefer drinkware that survives being dropped. It does not flatter fine wine, but it holds ale, water, broth, and the practical trust of rough hands.
flavorText: “Wooden cups forgive the floor.”

name: ceramic cup
shortDescription: A ceramic cup with a fired body and comfortable weight, common in homes, tea stalls, and taverns that want more refinement than wood without risking fine glass. Chips lower the price, but a sound glaze keeps buyers interested.
flavorText: “Clay remembers the kiln and tries not to remember the table edge.”

name: iron tankard
shortDescription: A hard-wearing iron tankard made for alehouses, guards, sailors, and drinkers who treat cups as tools. It is heavy, durable, and not especially elegant, which makes it ideal for establishments where elegance gets dented before closing.
flavorText: “An iron tankard survives the toast and the argument after it.”

name: large candelabra
shortDescription: A large candelabra with branching arms for candles, table light, ceremony, or the theatrical improvement of a dim hall. Its value depends on metalwork and presence, because rich buyers pay extra when shadows retreat dramatically.
flavorText: “One candle is light. Many candles become atmosphere.”

name: large houseplant
shortDescription: A large houseplant kept in a pot, valued for filling empty corners, softening stone rooms, and proving someone can afford to water decoration. It is awkward to transport but popular with estates, guild halls, and inns pretending to be calmer than they are.
flavorText: “Nothing says prosperity like keeping a leaf alive indoors.”

name: mirror
shortDescription: A polished mirror for bedrooms, dressing rooms, barber stalls, and anyone brave enough to consult glass before leaving the house. Good mirrors sell for clarity; poor ones sell for mercy, since not every buyer wants the exact truth.
flavorText: “A mirror is the most honest liar in the room.”

name: needle
shortDescription: A small sewing needle of plain metal, nearly worthless alone until cloth tears, buttons vanish, or a sail threatens to split. Tailors, sailors, surgeons, and careful travelers all keep spares because losing one can stop an entire repair.
flavorText: “Tiny steel holds whole coats together.”

name: small candelabra
shortDescription: A small candelabra sized for writing desks, bedside tables, shrines, and modest dinners that still deserve more than one flame. It offers a touch of ceremony without requiring a hall, a servant, or excessive polishing.
flavorText: “A small cluster of light can make a poor table feel invited.”

name: small houseplant
shortDescription: A small potted plant for windowsills, counters, desks, and rooms that need a living thing less demanding than a pet. It sells well as a gift, apology, decoration, or proof that the buyer believes tomorrow will still need watering.
flavorText: “Hope is sometimes sold in a clay pot.”

name: standing candelabra
shortDescription: A tall standing candelabra for halls, chapels, stage corners, and grand rooms where light should rise from the floor like an attendant. It is furniture, lamp, and status marker at once, especially when polished before guests arrive.
flavorText: “Tall light makes even ordinary rooms rehearse grandeur.”

name: vase
shortDescription: A vase made to hold flowers, reeds, painted branches, or simply the idea that a table deserved something taller. Its worth depends on shape, glaze, and whether it can stand empty without looking accused.
flavorText: “A vase is useful only when it convinces people it is not empty.”

name: crystal ball
shortDescription: A polished crystal ball used by fortune tellers, court mystics, collectors, and frauds with excellent lighting. Whether it truly reveals anything is debated, but it reflects faces beautifully, which is often enough to begin a convincing prophecy.
flavorText: “The future is clearest when customers pay before asking.”

name: scarce diamond tooth key
shortDescription: A rare key cut with diamond-like teeth, too fine for ordinary locks and too memorable for careless pockets. Collectors value its shine, locksmiths value its precision, and secretive buyers value the uncomfortable question of what it opens.
flavorText: “Some keys look expensive enough to make the door jealous.”

name: notable key heart
shortDescription: A notable heart-shaped key with symbolic craft and enough romance to make practical locksmiths sigh. It is bought for chests, vows, puzzle boxes, and ceremonies where unlocking something matters less than being seen unlocking it.
flavorText: “Heart keys rarely open the thing their owners hope for.”

name: old key
shortDescription: An old key darkened by handling, age, and the mystery of misplaced locks. It may be useless, priceless, or merely waiting for the right cellar door, which is why collectors and adventurers both keep buying them in small, hopeful lots.
flavorText: “Old keys sell because every forgotten door feels possible.”

name: two tooth key
shortDescription: A simple two-tooth key with a practical cut, suited to common chests, interior locks, and old mechanisms without much patience for elegance. It is not grand, but it is easy to copy, carry, and lose at exactly the wrong time.
flavorText: “Two teeth are enough if the lock is willing.”

name: location tracker amulet
shortDescription: A magical amulet said to tug, warm, or shine when turned toward a marked place or person. Scouts, jealous nobles, debt collectors, and anxious families all ask for it, though wise merchants avoid promising how precise the magic feels.
flavorText: “Finding is easy to sell and difficult to guarantee.”

name: amulet of bones
shortDescription: An amulet strung with carved bone pieces, worn for ancestral memory, grave rites, or protection from things people prefer not to name at market. Its value rises when the carving is clean and the seller does not explain the source too eagerly.
flavorText: “Bone remembers shape after flesh has forgotten business.”

name: blue cameo amulet
shortDescription: A blue cameo amulet with a raised portrait or profile set into polished backing, more refined than most charms and easier to wear at court. It appeals to collectors, sentimental heirs, and merchants who understand that faces make jewelry feel personal.
flavorText: “A tiny portrait can make a stranger feel inherited.”

name: market amulet of strength
shortDescription: A market-grade strength amulet sold to porters, guards, laborers, and hopeful youths who want courage to feel heavier in the hand. It may not turn weakness into might, but belief alone can lift a surprising number of crates.
flavorText: “Strength charms sell best beside things nobody wants to carry.”

name: blue celtic amulet
shortDescription: A blue Celtic-style amulet woven with looping knots, cool enamel, and old-pattern charm work. It is bought for beauty, luck, lineage, or the pleasing idea that a knot can trap misfortune before it reaches the wearer.
flavorText: “Blue knots promise calm even when the road disagrees.”

name: red celtic amulet
shortDescription: A red Celtic-style amulet with interlaced knotwork and a warmer, bolder presence than its blue cousin. Travelers wear it for passion, protection, and show, while jewelers praise how red enamel convinces buyers a charm has a pulse.
flavorText: “A red knot looks like luck tied while angry.”

name: yellow celtic amulet
shortDescription: A yellow Celtic-style amulet bright with looping patterns, worn for fortune, health, trade luck, or simply cheerful ornament. It catches torchlight well, making it popular with market sellers who know brightness can do half the bargaining.
flavorText: “Golden color makes even modest metal speak confidently.”

name: green crystal amulet
shortDescription: A green crystal amulet associated with growth, healing, forest roads, and the sort of luck that smells faintly of rain. Herbalists, hunters, and rural nobles favor it, especially when the crystal is clear enough to suggest honest magic.
flavorText: “Green stones always claim they know what wants to live.”

name: red crystal amulet
shortDescription: A red crystal amulet with a warm inner glow, bought for vitality, courage, romance, and other expensive forms of confidence. It is easy to sell under lamplight, where the stone seems to hold a tiny ember for the wearer.
flavorText: “Red crystal turns wanting into something that looks enchanted.”

name: white crystal amulet
shortDescription: A white crystal amulet prized for purity, clarity, warding rites, and clean formal dress. Priests, healers, brides, and cautious travelers all ask for it, though merchants know pale stones must be wrapped carefully or every smudge becomes a discount.
flavorText: “White crystal sells the dream of an untouched path.”

name: gray cylinder amulet
shortDescription: A gray cylindrical amulet with a restrained shape, favored by scholars, monks, and practical mystics who distrust gaudy stones. It looks plain at first glance, which suits buyers who prefer their charms quiet, durable, and difficult to mock.
flavorText: “Some magic wears gray so fools leave it alone.”

name: cyan eye amulet
shortDescription: A cyan eye amulet made to watch roads, doorways, envy, and the invisible attention of strangers. Its bright gaze is unsettling enough to feel useful, which is why travelers hang it where others cannot avoid noticing it.
flavorText: “The best ward is sometimes a stare nobody invited.”

name: green eye amulet
shortDescription: A green eye amulet associated with forest spirits, watchfulness, and luck that sees trouble before it arrives. It is popular among hunters, herbalists, and caravan guards who want a charm that appears alert even when they are not.
flavorText: “A green eye watches the road like it grew there.”

name: magenta eye amulet
shortDescription: A magenta eye amulet with a striking gaze, more exotic than common wards and far harder to ignore. Fortune tellers, performers, and eccentric nobles favor it because protection feels more convincing when everyone else finds it slightly uncomfortable.
flavorText: “A strange eye keeps ordinary doubts at a distance.”

name: face of gold amulet
shortDescription: A gold-faced amulet shaped like a small mask or divine likeness, bought for favor, protection, vanity, or all three. It is valuable even before magic is considered, because gold has a talent for making belief look official.
flavorText: “When faith wears gold, merchants weigh both.”

name: market shrunken head amulet
shortDescription: A market shrunken head amulet sold as a grim ward, curiosity, or stage prop for buyers who want a charm with unsettling character. Most are crafted imitations, which is usually a relief and occasionally a disappointment.
flavorText: “The safest kind is fake. The most expensive kind is uncertain.”

name: green penta amulet
shortDescription: A green penta amulet marked with a five-point charm, linked by sellers to balance, growth, and tidy protection from messy fortune. It suits hedge mages and cautious traders who like their symbols clear and their luck renewable.
flavorText: “Five points make a small shape feel prepared.”

name: orange penta amulet
shortDescription: An orange penta amulet with a warm symbol and lively color, often sold for vigor, creativity, travel luck, or market boldness. It catches the eye quickly, which is useful for a charm that promises energy before proving anything.
flavorText: “Orange charms do not whisper; they arrive.”
